main_sections.title: I Cannot Remember My Mother
main_sections.poem_text:
I cannot remember my mother
only sometimes in the midst of my play
a tune seems to hover over my playthings,
the tune of some song that she used to
hum while rocking my cradle.

I cannot remember my mother
but when in the early autumn morning
the smell of the shiuli flowers floats in the air
the scent of the morning service in the temple
comes to me as the scent of my mother.

I cannot remember my mother
only when from my bedroom window I send
my eyes into the blue of the distant sky,
I feel that the stillness of
my mother's gaze on my face
has spread all over the sky.
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Rabindranath Tagore's three-stanza lyric traces the speaker's fragmented, sensory memory of a mother he lost too early to remember directly. Each stanza anchors a different sense: a hovering lullaby tune heard amid play (auditory); the scent of shiuli flowers in an autumn morning merging with the fragrance of temple worship (olfactory); the infinite stillness of the sky seen from a bedroom window that recalls the mother's gaze on the child's face (visual). The refrain 'I cannot remember my mother' — repeated as the opening of each stanza — paradoxically makes her presence pervasive. The poem's tone is tender and quietly melancholic, caught between loss and a deeper, instinctive belonging. Tagore employs imagery, symbolism (shiuli flowers as a symbol of the mother's gentle, devotional spirit; the sky as the eternal extension of her gaze), and refrain as its dominant devices.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Reflect and Respond + Reading for Appreciation + Check Your Understanding + Critical Reflection

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Reflect and Respond — I Work in pairs. Discuss the memories from your childhood that you remember. List them and share with your classmates and teacher.
Reflect and Respond — II Discuss how children's relationship with their mother can influence their emotions and memories.

Reflect and Respond — III Match the words given in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2. [hover, hum, cradle, shiuli, morning service, gaze — matched to: linger near a place / sing with lips closed / small bed for a baby / coral jasmine flower / temple rituals / look steadily for a long time]

question_bank.stem: Match: hover, hum, cradle, shiuli, morning service, gaze — to their meanings.
question_bank.table:
Column 1|Column 2
hover|(i) sing a tune with your lips closed
hum|(ii) a type of flower (coral jasmine)
cradle|(iii) look steadily for a long time
shiuli|(iv) rituals conducted in a temple
morning service|(v) linger or remain near a place
gaze|(vi) a small bed for a baby

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Check Your Understanding — I Fill in the blanks with appropriate words. Stanza 1: 1. The poet remembers his mother while he ___. 2. The poet remembers the ___ but not the ___. Setting: ___ (outdoor/indoor). Stanza 2: 1. The poet remembers his mother in the ___ season. 2. The poet remembers his mother by the smell of ___. Setting: ___ (outdoor/indoor). Stanza 3: 1. The poet feels that his mother ___ at him from the ___. Setting: ___ (outdoor/indoor).

question_bank.stem: Stanza 1 fill-in: The poet remembers his mother while he ___; the ___ but not the ___; Setting: ___ (outdoor/indoor).
Stanza 2 fill-in: poet remembers mother in the ___ season; by the smell of ___; Setting: ___ (outdoor/indoor).
Stanza 3 fill-in: poet feels that his mother ___ at him from the ___; Setting: ___ (outdoor/indoor).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Check Your Understanding — II The senses of olfactory (smell), auditory (hearing), and visual (sight) are three of the five basic senses in humans. Give examples of references to senses from the poem. Complete the table given below. [Table: Stanza / Lines from the Poem / Senses — with one example given for Stanza 3 (auditory) and Stanza 2 (olfactory marked)]

question_bank.stem: Complete the senses table: identify lines from Stanzas 1 and 2 referencing auditory/olfactory/visual senses.
question_bank.table:
Stanza|Lines from the Poem|Senses
1.|_____|_____
3.|...the tune of some song that she used to hum while rocking my cradle|_____
2.|_____|olfactory

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Check Your Understanding — III Read the poem silently once again and complete the following. 1. Two examples of alliteration from the poem are (i) ___ and (ii) ___. 2. An example of onomatopoeia is ___. 3. The poem uses imagery extensively. Explain. 4. Although the poem does not have a rhyme scheme, it is enjoyable because ___. 5. What is the tone of the poet? Why do you say so? 6. What impact does the title of the poem have on the overall mood of the poem? 7. The poet uses 'I cannot remember my mother' as a refrain because ___. 8. The poet uses symbolism to indicate the memory of his mother's presence. Identify the symbols used in the poem.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Critical Reflection — I Extract 1: 'I cannot remember my mother / only sometimes in the midst of my play / a tune seems to hover over my playthings,'

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence appropriately. The poet is reminded of his mother during his ___.

What is the primary emotion conveyed by the line 'a tune seems to hover over my playthings'? A. It enhances the joy of play. B. It disrupts the playtime atmosphere. C. It activates memories of the mother. D. It symbolises the carefree nature of childhood.
options: It enhances the joy of play.
It activates memories of the mother.
It symbolises the carefree nature of childhood.

question_bank.stem: In the context of the poem, what role does the hovering tune play during the speaker's playtime?
State whether the following sentence is true or false. The poet experiences the tune lingering over playthings only occasionally during playtime.
How could the poet feel his mother's presence, even though she isn't there?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Critical Reflection — I Extract 2: 'I feel that the stillness of / my mother's gaze on my face / has spread all over the sky.'

question_bank.stem: What does the poet suggest about the stillness of his mother's gaze spreading over the sky? A. The mother's gaze is physically present in the sky. B. The sky is a symbolic extension of the mother's presence. C. The sky mirrors the mother's emotions. D. The stillness is fleeting and unrelated to the mother.
options: The mother's gaze is physically present in the sky.
The sky is a symbolic extension of the mother's presence.
The sky mirrors the mother's emotions.
The stillness is fleeting and unrelated to the mother.

question_bank.stem: What emotion does the poet associate with the 'stillness' of his mother's gaze? A. a sense of grief B. a sense of anticipation C. a sense of nostalgia D. a sense of serenity
State whether the following sentence is true or false. The poet suggests that the mother's gaze has a tangible and visual effect on the sky.
What is the purpose of likening the mother's gaze to the sky?
Complete the sentence appropriately. The tone of the poet in the given extract is ___ because ___.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Critical Reflection — II Answer the following questions. 1. What is the emotional impact of the refrain, 'I cannot remember my mother'? 2. Interpret the connection between the poet's mother and the following: (i) shiuli flowers (ii) humming tune. 3. What role does nature play in the poet's description of the memory of his mother? 4. What can be inferred about the poet's perception of the mother–child relationship?

question_bank.stem: What is the emotional impact of the refrain, 'I cannot remember my mother'?
Interpret the connection between the poet's mother and the following: (i) shiuli flowers (ii) humming tune.
What role does nature play in the poet's description of the memory of his mother?
What can be inferred about the poet's perception of the mother–child relationship?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Work in pairs to classify the sensory words given in the box below. One example has been done for you. [Words: rustle, sticky, glowing, hiss, rough, chilled, aroma, gigantic, stale, sizzle, minuscule, scent, gloomy, deafening, vibrant, pungent, squeaky, stinky, crimson, fluffy, fragrant, smooth, slimy, ear-splitting, hairy — classify into Visual / Auditory / Olfactory / Tactile]

question_bank.stem: Classify sensory words into Visual, Auditory, Olfactory, Tactile categories.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II Fill in the blanks with sensory words in the box for the following passage written by Sarojini Naidu. There are two extra words. [Words: scarlet, shrill, sizzle, perfumes, flaming, scents, sweetness, essence, smooth — fill 7 blanks in Sarojini Naidu passage about a March morning]

question_bank.stem: Fill in 7 blanks in the Sarojini Naidu March morning passage with appropriate sensory words from the given box (two words are extra).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III Write numbers against each picture with the phrases that describe them with their sensorial associations. One example has been done for you. [6 pictures; 6 phrases: 1. beats of music echo in air, 2. melody of soothing scent dancing in the air, 3. fragrant breeze of blooming buds, 4. gentle lullaby a soft melody, 5. colourful sky painting a lively sight, 6. attractive canvas painting the horizon]

question_bank.stem: Match 6 phrases to their corresponding pictures using sensory associations.

listening.transcript_text: Four speakers share childhood memories. Speaker (i): As a child, when disheartened, the speaker's grandfather's advice about keeping morale high echoes in his mind. Grandfather would ask 'who is the bravest one?' and the speaker always shouted 'Me'. The voice and belief still uplift the speaker when feeling low. Speaker (ii): Childhood was about liberty — chasing butterflies, making paper boats in rain, playing on sandy beaches, returning home muddy to escape scolding. These carefree joys echo as simple, cherished memories. Speaker (iii): Cherished preparatory-school days include a grandma's sweet tolerance. Mornings meant sitting on grandpa's lap demanding comic-strip tales from newspapers, then pretending to retell stories in the kitchen and ending up babbling, forgetting the real narratives — beautiful memories that linger clearly. Speaker (iv): Childhood at the beach with parents is a cherished memory, until the family moved when the speaker was in Grade 1. Returning after a decade, the once serene beach is now crowded and the carefree days are replaced by a sense of distance within the family, highlighting a shift in generational connections.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I You will listen to four short extracts of people expressing their childhood memories. As you listen, match each statement 1–6 to each speaker (i)–(iv). There are two statements that you do not need. (Transcript for teacher on page 266) [Statements: 1. The precious memories by the seaside are not the same anymore. 2. My grandfather's encouragement influences me in moments of difficulty. 3. The school days are a source of recollection to stay in touch. 4. Childhood days are about freedom and ordinary pleasures in outdoor activities. 5. Memories of school days are grandparents and my funny tales. 6. Parents urge us to be adventurous and discover nature but with limitations.]

question_bank.stem: Match statements 1–6 to speakers (i)–(iv). Two statements are not needed.

Statement|Speaker
1. The precious memories by the seaside are not the same anymore.|___

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Work in pairs. Think of an object, song, or a place that is memorable for you. Take turns to speak about it using the prompts given below: Introduce it briefly; Describe it in detail including sensory details; Narrate the specific memory associated with it; Explain how it has impacted your life; Summarise why this memory is meaningful to you even now. [Sentence starters provided: 'During my childhood…', 'Growing up, I fondly remember…', 'I have a clear memory of…', etc.]

I Imagine you had been on a school trip to a scenic place which appealed to all your senses. Write a diary entry describing the place and why it was a memorable experience. Contents: begin with day, date, and time; use only first person (I, my); capture sensory details; express emotions; focus on significant moments and experiences; conclude with reflection. [Note on diary format: Anne Frank and Zlata Filipovic began with 'Dear diary'; Samuel Pepys did not.]

I You read about the shiuli flowers from the poem. Gather more information on unique flowers of India — where they are found, how they look, what smell they emit, and what the historical significance is, if any. [Example given: Kurinji/Neelakurinji that blooms once in twelve years.] Add more flowers to this list. [Shiuli flowers info provided: known as Parijat/Parijata/Harsingar; revered for ceremonial purposes; release a sweet fragrance; associated with numerous Indian legends; bloom exclusively during the night.]

II You must have read poems and songs related to the theme of family, friends and their reminiscences, in English and also in your own language. Collect these poems and songs, and share with your classmates and teacher. Now, read and enjoy the poem given below. [Poem: 'I Remember, I Remember' by Thomas Hood — speaker remembers the house where they were born, the little window where the sun came peeping in at morn, roses red and white, violets and lily cups, lilacs where the robin built, and the laburnum set by a brother on his birthday, noting 'The tree is living yet!' — a nostalgic recollection of a childhood home and garden, contrasted with a wish that the night had taken the speaker's breath away, suggesting present-day melancholy.]
